narrative:
## State of the Story: Fatal Shootout Between Cuban Forces and Florida-Registered Speedboat

On Wednesday, February 25, 2026, Cuban border guards shot and killed four people aboard a Florida-registered speedboat in Cuban territorial waters, with six others wounded in what both sides describe as an armed confrontation [1][2][6][9][10][11].

### Key Facts Established

**Location and Timing**: The incident occurred Wednesday morning approximately one nautical mile northeast of the El Pino channel, near Cayo Falcones in Cuba's Villa Clara province [6][9][12][13]. The speedboat bore Florida registration number FL7726SH [12][13].

**Casualties**: Four people aboard the speedboat were killed and six wounded [1][2][6][9][10][11]. A Cuban border guard commander was also injured in the exchange [1][2][6][9][11]. The wounded from the speedboat were evacuated and received medical assistance [6][9][11].

**Sequence of Events**: Cuban authorities claim their five-member border guard unit approached the speedboat for identification when those aboard "opened fire" first, injuring the Cuban commander [6][9][11][12]. Cuban forces then returned fire, resulting in the casualties [11][12].

### Cuban Government Claims

Cuba has made several specific allegations about the incident's nature and participants:

- The 10 people aboard planned "an infiltration with terrorist aims" and were "Cuban nationals residing in the US" [1]
- Most had "prior records involving criminal and violent activity" [1]
- The boat carried "handguns, assault rifles and improvised explosive devices" along with "other tactical gear" [1]
- Cuba identified several individuals, including two allegedly wanted for terrorism-related activities: Amijail Sánchez González and Leordan Enrique Cruz Gómez [2][5]
- One fatality was identified as Michel Ortega Casanova [2]
- An 11th person, Duniel Hernández Santos, was arrested on Cuban territory and allegedly "confessed to being part of the alleged plot" [1][5]

### US Response and Investigation

Secretary of State Marco Rubio, speaking from St. Kitts and Nevis, confirmed the US was investigating the "highly unusual" incident [1][2][6]. Rubio stated it was not a US government operation and no US personnel were involved [2][6]. He emphasized the US would not rely solely on Cuban accounts: "We're not going to base our conclusions on what they've [Cuba] told us" [2][10].

Florida Attorney General James Uthmeier ordered a state investigation, declaring "The Cuban government cannot be trusted, and we will do everything in our power to hold these communists accountable" [2][6][8][10][12].

### Conflicting Accounts and Unverified Claims

While Cuban authorities provide detailed allegations about the participants' backgrounds and intentions, sources note that "No evidence was offered to support the claim the victims were exiled Cubans tasked by the U.S. to infiltrate the island nation" [5]. 

One unnamed US official told The New York Times the speedboat was "part of a flotilla to get relatives out of Cuba" [10][12][13], presenting a different potential motive than Cuba's terrorism allegations.

### Historical Context

The incident occurs during heightened US-Cuba tensions and comes "almost 30 years to the day since Cuban defence forces shot down two small civilian planes belonging to Brothers to the Rescue" in 1996, killing four people [1][10]. The area has seen previous armed clashes, including incidents in 2022 during Cuba's migration crisis [2][7][10].

### Ongoing Developments

US diplomats were working to gain access to determine if any victims were American citizens or permanent residents [6]. Cuban authorities continue investigating to "fully clarify the events" [9][11][12]. The BBC attempted to verify the speedboat's registration details but found no ownership information in available databases [1].
